title: 10th Amendment Restoration Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill would create a group of nine people to look at all federal government agencies and decide which ones should be closed down. The group would check if each agency has powers that the Constitution actually gives to the federal government. They would spend about five years studying the agencies, hold public meetings, and then tell Congress which agencies they think should be shut down. Congress would then vote on whether to close those agencies using special quick voting rules.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, and in addition to the Committee on Rules, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of su

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Ten Amendment Restoration Act of Two Thousand Twenty Five.

sections.title: Section 2  Establishment
sections.text: This law creates a group called the Constitutional Government Review Commission. This group will look at each federal agency (government department) to decide if it should be shut down. The goal is to eliminate federal agencies that have powers the Constitution does not clearly give to the federal government.

sections.title: Section 3  Membership
sections.text: The Commission will have nine members. The President must choose all nine members within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed. The Senate must approve each member. Each member will serve for five years and one hundred eighty days after this law is passed, or five years after all members are approved by the Senate, whichever is later. The President will choose one member to be the Chair. This person must know a lot about the Constitution and how federal government power works. The Speaker of the House, the House minority leader, the Senate majority leader, and the Senate minority leader will each give the President a list of candidates for the Commission. These candidates must understand the original meaning of the Constitution. The President must pick two members from each list. If the President thinks someone on a list does not meet the requirements, the President can ask for a new list. Commission members will be paid daily at the rate for level four federal executives, except the Chair who gets paid at level three. Members will also get money for travel expenses. The Commission can meet whenever it wants, but it must hold public meetings at least twice each year. All Commission meetings must be open to the public.

sections.title: Section 4  Duties
sections.text: The Commission will review the laws that created each federal agency and the powers those agencies use. The goal is to find agencies that have powers the Constitution does not clearly give to the federal government. The Constitution says any powers not given to the federal government belong to the states or the people. The Commission must create a method for doing these reviews and publish it in the Federal Register and on their website. They can ask for public comments on this method before making it final. After reviewing an agency, the Commission will decide whether to recommend shutting it down immediately. A majority of Commission members must vote to make this recommendation. No vote can happen until all Commission members are approved by the Senate. The Commission will work with the Comptroller General (the government's top accountant) to estimate how much money would be saved by shutting down an agency. The Commission will then recommend how to give this saved money to the states in lump sums to help them take over the responsibilities. The President, any member of Congress, any government employee at any level, or any member of the public can ask the Commission to review a specific agency. These requests must identify the agency, explain why it should be reviewed, and include any helpful information. The Commission must put these requests on their website within one week. The Commission must publish notices of all public meetings, hearings, and recommendations in the Federal Register and on their website before they happen. After meetings and hearings, they must publish detailed reports about what happened. Starting one year after all Commission members are approved, the Commission must send Congress an annual report about their activities and recommendations. This can include a proposed bill with specific legal language. When the Commission's time is up, they must send Congress a final report summarizing everything they did and recommended, including a list of agencies to shut down and how to distribute savings to states. This can also include a recommendation about whether Congress should create a new commission. The Commission must create a public website that uses current technology, provides information in standard formats, and allows public comments. All required information must be published quickly and be free to access. Records of public meetings must be posted within one week. All public comments and submissions must be published on the website. Notices of meetings must be posted at least one week before they happen.

sections.title: Section 5  Expedited Procedures for Consideration of Commission Recommendations
sections.text: This section creates special rules for how Congress will consider bills based on Commission recommendations. A Commission bill is one that contains the legal language from either the annual report or final report submitted by the Commission. The Senate majority leader or their designee must introduce the bill in the Senate within five legislative days, and the Speaker of the House or their designee must do the same in the House. In the House of Representatives, the Commission bill goes immediately on the calendar when introduced. Within thirty legislative days, someone can move to consider the bill. This motion cannot be debated, all objections are waived, and it cannot be reconsidered. When the House considers the bill, it is treated as already read, all objections are waived, and there can be ten hours of debate split equally between supporters and opponents. No amendments are allowed, and the vote to reconsider is not allowed. The bill passes with a majority vote of members present. In the Senate, the Commission bill also goes immediately on the calendar. Within thirty legislative days, someone can move to consider it, even if a previous motion failed. This motion cannot be debated, postponed, or reconsidered. If it passes, the bill stays as unfinished business until resolved. When the Senate considers the bill, all objections are waived, debate is limited to thirty hours split between majority and minority leaders, no amendments or other motions are allowed, and the bill passes with a majority vote. If one house passes its version and then receives the other house's version, they must vote on the other house's version for final passage. If the President vetoes the bill, Senate debate on the veto message is limited to ten hours. These rules are part of each house's procedures but can be changed like any other rule.

sections.title: Section 6  Director, Staff, and Experts and Consultants
sections.text: The Commission will hire a Director who gets paid at the rate for level five federal executives. With Commission approval, the Director can hire and fire additional staff and set their pay. The Director can hire people without following normal government hiring rules, and can pay them without following normal government pay scales, but no one can be paid more than a GS-fifteen government employee. After talking with the Commission Chair, the head of any federal agency can assign their employees to help the Commission. The Comptroller General must provide help to the Commission, including assigning employees, based on an agreement with the Commission. Congress and states can also provide help, including assigning employees, based on agreements with the Commission. The Commission can hire temporary experts or consultants by contract if they have the money.

sections.title: Section 7  Powers and Authorities
sections.text: The Commission can hold public hearings to consider facts or legal issues related to their work. The Commission can get information and documents directly from any federal agency. When the Commission Chair requests information, the agency head must provide it as soon as possible, but no later than two weeks after the request. The Commission can issue subpoenas (legal orders) requiring people to attend hearings and bring evidence. These can be served anywhere in the United States. If someone refuses to obey a subpoena, the Commission can ask a federal court to order that person to comply. Disobeying a court order can be punished as contempt of court. Subpoenas must be served following the same rules as federal court subpoenas. The Commission can lease office space and buy equipment if they have the money.

sections.title: Section 8  Authorization of Appropriations
sections.text: Congress can appropriate (set aside) whatever money is needed for the Commission to do its work, but no more than thirty million dollars. This money stays available until it is spent or the Commission ends, whichever happens first.

sections.title: Section 9  Applicability of Chapter 10 of Title 5, United States Code
sections.text: Unless this law says otherwise, the Commission must follow the rules in chapter ten of title five of the United States Code (rules about advisory committees). The Commission will not be controlled by any Advisory Committee Management Officer. Any subcommittee of the Commission will be treated like the Commission itself for these rules. Passing this law meets the charter requirements for the Commission.

sections.title: Section 10  Termination
sections.text: The Commission will end five years and one hundred eighty days after this law is passed, or five years after all Commission members start their terms, whichever is later.

sections.title: Section 11  Definitions
sections.text: In this law, agency means what that word means in section five hundred fifty one of title five of the United States Code (basically any federal government department or office). State means any of the fifty states, the District of Columbia, any commonwealth, territory, or possession of the United States, and any federally recognized Indian Tribe. Unfunded mandate means what the term federal mandate means in section four hundred twenty one of the Congressional Budget Act of Nineteen Seventy Four (basically when the federal government requires something but does not pay for it).
